topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo in the quiet office. The subject line was innocuous, but the content chilled me to the bone: a notification that my piece, the one I’d poured months of sleepless nights into, had been published under the name of a complete stranger. My hands started to shake so badly I had to grip the edge of my desk to steady them. I reread the text, my eyes blurring slightly. It was a clumsy, almost mocking imitation of my prose. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling my breath shallow. Who could have done this? The realization that my intellectual property was being stolen, repackaged, felt like a sudden, suffocating vacuum. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked toward the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, every muscle in my body tense and coiled.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the words swimming before my eyes. It was a grotesque parody of my voice, a cheap knock-off masquerading as my own work. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room spin slightly. I pressed my palms flat against my eyes, trying to force the image away, but the text refused to dissolve. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming. I backed away from the computer, stumbling slightly as I bumped into the filing cabinet. My mouth went dry, and a low, shaky sound escaped my throat. "No," I whispered, the word barely audible, thick with disbelief and a rising sense of dread. I looked around the empty workspace, suddenly finding the familiar surroundings alien and menacing. The silence in the room amplified the frantic thumping in my chest, a relentless rhythm signaling utter disorientation.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I froze mid-sip of my coffee. The lukewarm liquid felt suddenly heavy in my throat. I slowly lowered the mug, the clink of ceramic against the saucer sounding deafening in the sudden stillness. My vision tunneled slightly; the edges of the room seemed to sharpen into harsh, unwelcome lines. I started pacing, a tight, jerky motion that served no purpose other than to expend the nervous energy churning inside me. Every time I glanced at the publication details, a distinct tremor ran through my hands, making it difficult to hold the paper steady. A deep, unsettling cold settled in my core, a sensation that made my skin prickle. I stopped pacing abruptly, leaning against the wall, breathing in short, sharp gasps as I fought to regulate the sudden, overwhelming surge of alarm.
